narrative: As a Hispanic male applying to law school, you've shared your undergraduate GPA of 2.90, an LSAT score of 37, middle family income bracket, and plans to attend full-time, along with your school decile rankings from Year 1 at the 3rd decile and Year 3 at the 1st decile. The admissions model, trained on data from thousands of past students' paths to the bar exam, predicted a 55.5% chance that you would fail the bar on your first try—enough to tip the decision against your application, as the school prioritizes those likely to pass and succeed in practice. This prediction hinges on just a few key aspects of your profile, with your Year 3 school decile ranking standing out as the biggest factor pushing the model toward expecting failure, since landing in the bottom 1st decile signals consistently lower performance compared to peers who typically rank much higher, around the 5th or 6th decile on average. Your LSAT score, while solid at 37 and slightly above the average of 36.7, worked in your favor as the second-most influential element, nudging the prediction toward success because it shows strong potential in the skills tested there. Meanwhile, your undergraduate GPA of 2.90, below the typical 3.21 average, provided some positive counterbalance but with less sway, as it still hints at room for academic growth. Other details like your full-time plans, which align with most students at 92.7%, and middle-income background played smaller roles overall. In essence, your low Year 3 decile outweighed the boosts from your LSAT and GPA, setting you apart from applicants whose stronger late-stage grades predict smoother bar passage. This isn't a judgment on your determination or background—many from similar Hispanic communities overcome these metrics through targeted effort. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to push it higher, bolstering your recent grades through post-bac courses or community college to climb those deciles, and gathering strong recommendation letters that highlight your resilience. Reapplying after addressing these could flip the model's view, and exploring bridge programs or schools with holistic reviews might open doors sooner. You're clearly driven; channeling that into these steps will make a real difference.
